prompts.label: Hello World project structure
prompts.text:
I'm new to Python. Set up a minimal project structure for learning.

- main.py that prints Hello, World
- Explain how to run it with python main.py
- Mention Python 3.11+ and using a virtual environment in 3–5 steps
- Add a short if __name__ == "__main__": block and explain why it's used

Do not use frameworks yet.

prompts.label: CLI script with argparse
prompts.text:
Create a beginner-friendly command-line script using argparse.

- Command: greet with optional --name (default World) and --shout flag (uppercase output)
- File: greet.py
- Show example terminal commands to run it
- Add basic error handling for unknown flags (argparse handles this)

Explain each part of argparse in simple terms.

prompts.label: Read and write CSV
prompts.text:
Teach me CSV in Python with a small example.

- Create sample data: name, email (3 rows)
- Script write_csv.py to write contacts.csv
- Script read_csv.py to read and print rows
- Use the csv module from the standard library

Include how to run both scripts and one common mistake to avoid.

prompts.label: Flask Hello World API
prompts.text:
I'm starting with Flask. Create the smallest useful web app.

- app.py with GET / returning Hello from Flask as plain text or HTML
- requirements.txt with Flask version pinned
- Steps: create venv, pip install, flask run
- Add a second route GET /health returning {"status": "ok"}

Keep files minimal and explain what each line does.

prompts.label: Django project first steps
prompts.text:
I'm a beginner with Django. Guide me through creating a new project.

- Commands: django-admin startproject mysite and startapp pages
- One view on / that renders a simple Hello Django template
- List every file you create or change and why
- settings.py: register the app
- Use Django 5.x style if possible

Assume I already have Python and pip. Do not skip migrations explanation.

prompts.label: Virtual env and requirements.txt
prompts.text:
Explain Python virtual environments like I'm new.

- Step-by-step: create venv, activate on Windows and macOS/Linux
- Create requirements.txt with requests and python-dotenv as example
- pip install -r requirements.txt
- Small script that loads a variable from .env (use python-dotenv)

Include a .gitignore snippet for venv and .env.

prompts.label: Calculator + pytest tests
prompts.text:
Build a tiny calculator module for learning tests.

- calculator.py: functions add, subtract, multiply, divide (divide by zero raises ValueError)
- test_calculator.py using pytest with at least 5 tests
- Show commands: pip install pytest, pytest -v

Explain the arrange-act-assert pattern in one test example.

prompts.label: List comprehensions practice
prompts.text:
Give me a short lesson on list comprehensions in Python.

- Start with a for-loop version, then rewrite as comprehension
- Examples: squares of 1–10, even numbers only, lowercase a list of strings
- One dict comprehension example
- 3 exercises with solutions hidden in collapsible comments or at the end

No external libraries.

prompts.label: HTTP GET with requests
prompts.text:
Show how to call a public HTTP API with the requests library.

- fetch_posts.py: GET from https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1
- Print title and body
- Handle timeout and HTTP errors with try/except
- requirements.txt with requests

Explain status codes 200 vs 404 briefly.

prompts.label: Error handling try/except
prompts.text:
Teach try/except with a small interactive script.

- Ask the user for two numbers in the terminal
- Parse with float(), compute division
- Catch ValueError (bad input) and ZeroDivisionError
- Use else and finally in a simple, readable way

Provide the full script and when to use specific except vs bare except (and why to avoid bare except).

prompts.label: pathlib for file paths
prompts.text:
I'm done with string paths and want pathlib on a small script.
Show how to create Path objects, join segments safely, check exists/is_file, and read/write UTF-8 text.
Contrast Path with os.path in one paragraph for beginners.
Include a tiny CLI script that counts lines in every .txt under a folder (non-recursive is fine).
Mention one Windows-specific edge case Path helps with.

prompts.label: logging instead of print
prompts.text:
Replace scattered print debugging with the logging module in a 50-line script.
Explain log levels DEBUG/INFO/WARNING/ERROR and when each fits.
Show basicConfig with a readable format string and how to log exceptions with exc_info.
Describe how to switch verbosity with an environment variable pattern.
Warn about one common misconfiguration in libraries that override logging.

prompts.label: datetime and time zones safely
prompts.text:
Teach me datetime basics without the classic naive/aware foot-gun.
Demonstrate UTC now, converting a fixed instant to a named zone with zoneinfo (Python 3.11+).
Format an ISO-8601 string suitable for logs.
Highlight why storing local wall-clock times in a database is risky.
Give a 5-line example parsing user input defensively.

prompts.label: contextlib.closing and custom context
prompts.text:
Deepen my understanding of context managers beyond open().
Show @contextmanager from contextlib for setup/teardown around a temporary file path.
Explain when try/finally is still clearer than a context manager.
Provide a second micro-example using closing() on a resource with a .close() method.
End with a rule of thumb for beginners about exceptions inside __enter__/__exit__.

prompts.label: dataclasses for simple records
prompts.text:
Model a small User and Order record with @dataclass.
Compare field(default_factory=list) vs a mutable default mistake.
Show when frozen=True is helpful for immutability-lite.
Add __post_init__ validation for a positive price.
Explain how this differs from a TypedDict or Pydantic when I'm just learning.

prompts.label: tomllib read project metadata
prompts.text:
I see pyproject.toml files everywhere and want to read one from Python 3.11+ using tomllib.
Write a tiny function load_project_name(path) returning the project name from [project].
Handle missing sections with clear errors suited to beginners.
Note TOML types vs JSON mental model in two sentences.
Show how I'd print the version field if present.

prompts.label: urllib.request GET without requests
prompts.text:
Sometimes I can't install requests yet. Walk me through urllib.request.urlopen.
Fetch a small JSON public endpoint, read bytes, decode UTF-8, json.loads into a dict.
Set a timeout and catch HTTPError/URLError with friendly messages.
Contrast readability vs requests in one honest paragraph.
Add a comment about User-Agent politeness.

prompts.label: itertools.groupby reporting
prompts.text:
Explain why itertools.groupby requires sorted input for many use cases.
Build a toy report: given a list of dict rows with category, group and print subtotals.
Warn about iterator exhaustion pitfalls with groupby.
Offer an alternative using collections.defaultdict for confused beginners.
Keep the whole example under ~25 lines.

prompts.label: typing.Protocol structural duck typing
prompts.text:
Introduce typing.Protocol for "anything with a read()".
Show a function parse_payload(source: Readable) that accepts pathlib.Path.open() objects and io.StringIO.
Explain @runtime_checkable cautiously.
Contrast nominal inheritance vs structural typing.
Provide a minimal mypy-friendly example without advanced generics.

prompts.label: zipfile backups mini exercise
prompts.text:
Write a beginner script that bundles a handful of *.csv files into a dated zip archive using zipfile.
Include Path.glob, arcname basename handling, and a dry-run flag printed to the console.
Describe security warnings about unpacking untrusted zips at a high level.
Explain how I'd run this on Windows and macOS similarly.
Finish with ideas for scheduling later without implementing cron.

prompts.label: Decorators and functools.wraps
prompts.text:
Teach Python decorators from zero with a practical example.

- Start with a function that prints how long another function takes
- Rewrite it as @timer decorator using functools.wraps so __name__ stays correct
- Show stacking two decorators and the call order
- One paragraph on when decorators beat plain function calls

Keep code under 40 lines and runnable.

prompts.label: Generators and yield
prompts.text:
Explain generators vs returning a full list in memory.

- Function read_lines_lazy(path) yielding stripped lines one at a time
- Compare memory use with list version in one sentence
- Show a generator expression filtering lines containing a keyword
- Mention when yield from helps with nested generators

Include a tiny main block that prints first 3 matches only.

prompts.label: asyncio hello and gather
prompts.text:
I'm new to asyncio in Python 3.11+.

- Minimal script: async def main(), asyncio.run(main())
- Two fake tasks with await asyncio.sleep and asyncio.gather
- Explain event loop in 3 bullets for beginners
- When asyncio helps vs when threads or sync code is simpler

No external libraries; keep under 35 lines.

prompts.label: sqlite3 CRUD mini app
prompts.text:
Build a tiny notes app using only sqlite3 from the standard library.

- Create notes.db with a tasks table (id, title, done)
- Functions: add_task, list_tasks, mark_done
- Use context manager for connection and parameterized queries (? placeholders)
- Show CLI examples or a minimal input loop

Warn about SQL injection if someone tries string formatting for SQL.

prompts.label: subprocess run external commands
prompts.text:
Show safe patterns for running external programs with subprocess.

- subprocess.run with list args (not shell=True) for git --version or similar
- Capture stdout/stderr as text, check returncode
- Timeout handling and CalledProcessError
- One paragraph why shell=True is risky for user input

Works on Windows and Unix; note path differences briefly.

prompts.label: enum.Enum for constants
prompts.text:
Replace magic strings with enum.Enum in a small user-status example.

- class Status(str, Enum) with ACTIVE, INACTIVE, PENDING
- Function that accepts Status and rejects invalid strings with clear error
- Show .value and comparing enums
- Mention auto() vs explicit values for beginners

Under 30 lines of code.

prompts.label: functools.lru_cache memoization
prompts.text:
Demonstrate functools.lru_cache on a slow pure function (e.g. naive Fibonacci or repeated JSON key lookup).

- Before/after timing with time.perf_counter
- Explain maxsize and when cache is wrong (mutable args, side effects)
- Show cache_info() and cache_clear()
- One line on functools.cache as unlimited variant

Runnable script only.

prompts.label: re module regex basics
prompts.text:
Teach the re module for beginners without overwhelming regex theory.

- re.search and re.findall on sample log lines
- Compile a pattern once with re.compile
- Simple validator function for a loose email pattern (not production-perfect)
- Explain raw strings r"..." and why greedy vs non-greedy matters in one example

Include 2–3 test strings and expected match results.

prompts.label: json load dump custom types
prompts.text:
Work with json module beyond basic dict/list.

- Serialize a dataclass or dict with datetime using default= handler
- Pretty-print with indent=2
- Load from a file with Path and json.loads safety note (size limits)
- Contrast json vs orjson in one sentence for later

Provide sample data.json and script read_write_json.py.

prompts.label: secrets for tokens and passwords
prompts.text:
Explain why random.random() is wrong for security tokens.

- secrets.token_urlsafe and secrets.choice for short codes
- Compare with uuid.uuid4 for session ids (when each fits)
- Never log secrets; environment variable pattern
- One paragraph on password hashing: use bcrypt/argon2 library, not DIY

Small script generating a reset token and API key placeholder.

prompts.label: hashlib file checksums
prompts.text:
Write sha256_file(path) reading in chunks so large files work.

- Use hashlib.sha256().update(block)
- CLI: python checksum.py file1 file2
- Optional compare two files for equality
- Mention blake2b as faster alternative in one line

Include how to verify a download if publisher gives a checksum string.

prompts.label: tempfile scratch directories
prompts.text:
Show tempfile.TemporaryDirectory and NamedTemporaryFile with context managers.

- Script that writes intermediate CSV parts then merges them
- Explain delete=True behavior and Windows file-lock caveat briefly
- Why not hardcode /tmp paths
- Cleanup guaranteed even on exception

Keep example under 25 lines.

prompts.label: shutil copy and disk usage
prompts.text:
Teach shutil for file operations beginners need.

- shutil.copy2 vs copy for metadata
- shutil.copytree with ignore= function skipping __pycache__
- shutil.disk_usage reporting free space in GB
- When to prefer pathlib operations instead

Small backup-folder demo with dry-run print only.

prompts.label: unittest.mock patch dependencies
prompts.text:
Introduce unittest.mock for isolating code that calls external APIs.

- Function fetch_title(url) using urllib or requests
- Test with @patch and MagicMock returning fake response
- assert_called_once_with and side_effect for errors
- Compare with pytest monkeypatch in 2 sentences

Use unittest or pytest style; pick one and stay consistent.

prompts.label: property getter setter validation
prompts.text:
Explain @property for controlled attribute access.

- class Product with private _price and property price that rejects negative values
- Optional setter with validation message
- When @property beats explicit get_price() methods
- Link to dataclasses field(validator=...) as modern alternative briefly

Include one test or assert example.

prompts.label: args kwargs unpacking
prompts.text:
Clarify *args and **kwargs for beginners.

- Function log_call(func, *args, **kwargs) that prints name and arguments then calls func
- Example with positional and keyword args
- Explain unpacking in calls: func(*items, **options)
- Common mistake: mutable default dict in signature

Short examples only, no classes yet.

prompts.label: abc abstract base classes
prompts.text:
Introduce abc.ABC and @abstractmethod with a Notifier interface.

- Abstract class with send(message)
- EmailNotifier and ConsoleNotifier implementations
- What happens if you instantiate ABC directly
- When ABC helps vs simple duck typing Protocol

Runnable demo in one file under 45 lines.

prompts.label: Counter and defaultdict
prompts.text:
Use collections.Counter and defaultdict for a word-frequency report.

- Read a text file, normalize case, split words
- Counter.most_common(10)
- defaultdict(list) grouping lines by first letter
- Compare with manual dict.setdefault

No external libs; sample paragraph included in prompt output.

prompts.label: heapq top-k largest
prompts.text:
Teach heapq with a leaderboard of scores.

- heapq.nlargest and nsmallest on a list of tuples (name, score)
- Push/pop pattern with heapify for streaming top 3
- When heap beats sorting entire list (big data intuition)
- One line on PriorityQueue wrapper if relevant

Small dataset example with printed ranking.

prompts.label: match case structural patterns
prompts.text:
Explain structural pattern matching (match/case) in Python 3.10+.

- Match int status codes 200, 404, default
- Second example matching dict shapes {"type": "error", "code": ...}
- Compare readability to if/elif chains
- Note: not a replacement for isinstance everywhere

Two concise examples with expected print output.

prompts.label: Type hints list dict optional
prompts.text:
Add gradual typing to a small module for learning.

- Functions with list[str], dict[str, int], str | None
- Typed def process(items: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]
- Run mypy or pyright one-liner command
- Explain why hints don't slow runtime
- Common beginner mistake: using list instead of list[str] in old code

Provide py.typed note only if packaging—skip heavy packaging.

prompts.label: Pydantic v2 model validation
prompts.text:
Create a Pydantic v2 model for API input validation.

- UserCreate with email, age (ge=18), name min length
- model_validate from dict and ValidationError pretty printing
- model_dump for JSON-ready dict
- Contrast with dataclass + manual checks

requirements.txt with pydantic>=2. Keep one file example.

prompts.label: pyproject.toml and pip install -e
prompts.text:
Walk through making a tiny installable package for learning.

- pyproject.toml with [project] name, version, dependencies
- src/mypkg/__init__.py and one function
- pip install -e .
- Import mypkg from REPL and run
- Explain src layout vs flat layout in 3 bullets

Do not publish to PyPI; local only.

prompts.label: black and ruff formatter linter
prompts.text:
Set up black and ruff on a messy sample script.

- pyproject.toml [tool.black] and [tool.ruff] minimal config
- Commands: black . and ruff check . --fix
- Show one before/after diff conceptually
- When to run in pre-commit (mention only, optional snippet)

Assume venv exists; list pip install lines.

prompts.label: threading basics and the GIL
prompts.text:
Introduce threading.Thread for I/O-bound work with honest GIL limits.

- 3 threads fetching fake URLs with time.sleep simulation
- threading.Lock protecting a shared counter
- Explain GIL in plain language (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound)
- When to look at multiprocessing or asyncio instead

Under 40 lines; no thread pools yet.

prompts.label: multiprocessing Pool map
prompts.text:
Show multiprocessing.Pool for CPU-heavy work on small data.

- if __name__ == "__main__" guard explained
- pool.map(square, range(10)) with timing compare to serial
- Windows spawn vs fork one sentence
- Warning: overhead dominates on tiny tasks

Runnable script with print timings.

prompts.label: Package __init__ and relative imports
prompts.text:
Explain Python packages beyond a single file.

- Folder myapp/ with __init__.py, utils.py, cli.py
- Relative import from .utils in cli.py
- python -m myapp.cli entry pattern
- Common ImportError causes when running wrong cwd

Tree diagram in comments; minimal working code.

prompts.label: slots and memory on many objects
prompts.text:
Teach __slots__ when creating many similar instances.

- Regular class vs __slots__ = ("x", "y") attribute restriction
- Mention dataclass(slots=True) in 3.10+
- Trade-off: no dynamic attributes, harder multiple inheritance
- When beginners should ignore this until profiling says otherwise

Micro-benchmark idea with sys.getsizeof, not rigorous benchmarking.

prompts.label: ipaddress networks and hosts
prompts.text:
Use ipaddress for network tasks instead of string hacking.

- ip_address, ip_network, host in subnet check
- Parse CIDR 192.168.1.0/24 and iterate usable hosts (small range only)
- IPv6 mention in one sentence
- Validate user input with try/except ValueError

Script: is_ip_allowed(ip, allowlist_of_networks).

prompts.label: Jupyter notebook first steps
prompts.text:
I'm starting with Jupyter for data exploration.

- Install: pip install jupyterlab (or notebook)
- Launch command and what opens in browser
- One markdown cell explaining headings, one code cell with import pandas as pd and df.head() on tiny inline CSV or dict data
- When notebooks beat .py scripts and when they don't (git diff, production)
- Export to .py tip

Keep notebook content described so I can recreate it; optional .ipynb structure outline.
